perturbation.original_text: (a) Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq., a t all times during the term of this Agreement and thereafter, the receiving party shall: (i) refrain from disclosing , directly or indirectly, any Confidential Information of the disclosing party to third parties , and hold all Confidential Information received from the disclosing party in trust and strict confidence, subject to the terms of this Agreement ; (ii) take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential information, but no less than reasonable care, to keep confidential the disclosing party’s Confidential Information and avoid d isclosure, loss or misuse of the Confidential Information; and (iii) refrain from using any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information for any purpose not specified her eunder (including , without limitation , in its own business or for its own benefit) without the prior written consent of the disclosing p arty.
perturbation.changed_text: (a)  At all times during the term of this Agreement and thereafter, the receiving party shall: (i) refrain from disclosing , directly or indirectly, any Confidential Information of the disclosing party to third parties , and hold all Confidential Information received from the disclosing party in trust and strict confidence, subject to the terms of this Agreement ; (ii) take reasonable security precautions to keep confidential the disclosing party’s Confidential Information and avoid d isclosure, loss or misuse of the Confidential Information; and (iii) refrain from using any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information for any purpose not specified her eunder (including , without limitation , in its own business or for its own benefit) without the prior written consent of the disclosing p arty.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq.,' eliminates an explicit acknowledgment of the public's right to access certain government records, and changes the 'reasonable security precautions' language.  By removing 'at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential information, but no less than reasonable care,' we weaken the protection of confidential information, potentially allowing the receiving party to use a lower standard of care than what would be considered compliant under Tennessee law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Tennessee Public Records Act ensures that government records are accessible to the public, but also provides certain exceptions for confidentiality. By removing reference to this Act, the agreement implies all information is confidential, which is incorrect. Also, Tennessee law mandates certain levels of security for protecting confidential information; the modified language weakens this.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 10-7-503 - Records open to public inspection Schedule of reasonable charges Costs. :: 2010 Tennessee Code :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Tennessee Code 2024 Tennessee Code 2023 Tennessee Code 2021 Tennessee Code 2010 Tennessee Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2010 Tennessee Code Title 10 - Public Libraries, Archives And Records Chapter 7 - Public Records Part 5 - Miscellaneous Provisions 10-7-503 - Records open to public inspection Schedule of reasonable charges Costs. 10-7-503. Records open to public inspection Schedule of reasonable charges Costs. (a) (1) As used in this part and title 8, chapter 4, part 6, public record or records or state record or records means all documents, papers, letters, maps, books, photographs, microfilms, electronic data processing files and output, films, sound recordings or other material, regardless of physical form or characteristics, made or received pursuant to law or ordinance or in connection with the transaction of official business by any governmental agency. (2) (A) All state, county and municipal records shall, at all times during business hours, which for public hospitals shall be during the business hours of their administrative offices, be open for personal inspection by any citizen of this state, and those in charge of the records shall not refuse such right of inspection to any citizen, unless otherwise provided by state law. (B) The custodian of a public record or the custodian's designee shall promptly make available for inspection any public record not specifically exempt from disclosure. In the event it is not practicable for the record to be promptly available for inspection, the custodian shall, within seven (7) business days: (i) Make the information available to the requestor; (ii) Deny the request in writing or by completing a records request response form developed by the office of open records counsel. The response shall include the basis for

perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the phrase 'Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq.,' which acknowledges the public's right to access certain government records. Scraped Law Snippet 1, particularly section (a)(2)(A), states that 'All state, county and municipal records shall, at all times during business hours... be open for personal inspection by any citizen of this state, and those in charge of the records shall not refuse such right of inspection to any citizen, unless otherwise provided by state law.' By removing the reference to the Tennessee Public Records Act, the agreement implies all information is confidential, which contradicts the law allowing public access to certain records. Additionally, the removal of 'at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential information, but no less than reasonable care,' weakens the protection of confidential information, as it removes a defined standard of care, and therefore, this constitutes a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (b) Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq.,w ithout the written consent of the disclos ing party, the receiving party will not disclose the disclosing party’s Confidential Information to any person other than the receiving party’s officers, directors, owners, employees, agents and representatives (i) with a valid need to know such Confidenti al Information and (ii) that have an enforceable agreement with the receiving party containing confidentiality obligations substantially similar to those terms and conditions applicable to the receiving party under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Without the written consent of the disclos ing party, the receiving party will not disclose the disclosing party’s Confidential Information to any person other than the receiving party’s officers, directors, owners, employees, agents and representatives (i) with a valid need to know such Confidenti al Information and (ii) that have an agreement with the receiving party containing confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq.,' we again omit the consideration for the public's right to access certain government records. Additionally, by changing 'enforceable agreement with the receiving party containing confidentiality obligations substantially similar to those terms and conditions applicable to the receiving party under this Agreement' to 'that have an agreement with the receiving party containing confidentiality obligations' we are weakening the burden the receiving party must place on those who have access to the information. This means the confidentiality obligations on those the receiving party shares the information with can have weaker confidentiality obligations than what the receiving party is bound to under the agreement. This change creates a legal risk in Tennessee.
perturbation.law_explanation: As stated before, the Tennessee Public Records Act ensures that government records are accessible to the public, but also provides certain exceptions for confidentiality. By removing reference to this Act, the agreement implies all information is confidential, which is incorrect. By weakening the wording of the confidentiality agreements required of those who have access to the information, it can potentially violate Tennessee law, because the agreement is not providing enough protection of the confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by removing the explicit reference to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10-7-5-1 et seq., which originally acknowledged the public's right to access certain government records. This omission implies that all information is confidential, contradicting the spirit and requirements of the Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically section 10-7-503(a)(2)(A), states that 'All state, county and municipal records shall, at all times during business hours...be open for personal inspection by any citizen of this state...unless otherwise provided by state law.' By removing the explicit reference to the Tennessee Public Records Act, the modified text implies that the confidentiality agreement supersedes the citizen's right to access records under the Act, which is a legal contradiction. Additionally, weakening the confidentiality obligations of those who receive the information from the receiving party makes the information less protected and more likely to be accessed, further undermining the intended protections under the law.

perturbation.original_text: (c) In the event that the r eceiving party is legally required to disclose any Confidential Information of the disclosing party , the receiving party shall give the disclosing party prompt written notice of such request or requirement so that the disclosing party may seek an appropriate protective order or other remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement, and the receiving party will reasonably cooperate with the disclosing party (at the disclosing party’s expense) to obtain such protective order. In the event that such protective order or other remedy is not obtained or the disclosing party waives compliance with the relevant provisions of this Agreement, the receiving party will furnish only that portion of the Confidential Information that is legally require d to be disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) In the event that the r eceiving party is legally required to disclose any Confidential Information of the disclosing party , the receiving party shall give the disclosing party notice of such request or requirement, and the receiving party will furnish that portion of the Confidential Information that is legally require d to be disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'prompt written' from the notice clause and removing 'so that the disclosing party may seek an appropriate protective order or other remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement, and the receiving party will reasonably cooperate with the disclosing party (at the disclosing party’s expense) to obtain such protective order. In the event that such protective order or other remedy is not obtained or the disclosing party waives compliance with the relevant provisions of this Agreement,' the receiving party is now free to provide information without giving the disclosing party enough notice to act on it.
perturbation.law_explanation: Tennessee law requires government entities to provide timely notice and an opportunity to respond before disclosing records that may be subject to an exception under the Public Records Act.  This omission creates a conflict with that requirement, preventing the disclosing party from adequately protecting its confidential information under the law. It also implies that the receiving party does not need to reasonably cooperate with the disclosing party which can go against Tennessee law requiring them to cooperate if needed.
perturbation.justification: The removal of 'prompt written' notice and the clause regarding protective orders significantly weakens the disclosing party's ability to protect its confidential information, which the Law Explanation suggests may conflict with Tennessee law. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not decipherable, the Law Explanation states Tennessee law requires government entities to provide timely notice and an opportunity to respond before disclosing records. The omission of 'prompt written' and the removal of language regarding protective orders directly undermines this timely notice and opportunity to respond. The modified text now allows the receiving party to disclose information without ensuring the disclosing party has sufficient time or means to seek legal remedies, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (e) Upon the disclosing party’s request , all documents, materials and other items containing the disclosing party’s Confidential Information (including without limitation, all originals, copies, and reproductions of the Confidential Information) shall be promptly returned to the disclosing party without retaining a copy thereof, an d the receiving party shall provide a certification , signed by an officer, as to the completeness of the return of such material s. Upon such request, the receiving party also shall destroy all documents, materials and other items embodying or summarizing the disclosing party’s Confidential Information in wha tever format, and shall provide a similar certification as to the completeness of the destruction of such materials. Compliance with this Subsection 2(e) shall not relieve the receiving party of its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: (e) Upon the disclosing party’s request , all documents, materials and other items containing the disclosing party’s Confidential Information shall be returned to the disclosing party. Compliance with this Subsection 2(e) shall not relieve the receiving party of its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'including without limitation, all originals, copies, and reproductions of the Confidential Information' and 'promptly returned to the disclosing party without retaining a copy thereof, an d the receiving party shall provide a certification , signed by an officer, as to the completeness of the return of such material s. Upon such request, the receiving party also shall destroy all documents, materials and other items embodying or summarizing the disclosing party’s Confidential Information in wha tever format, and shall provide a similar certification as to the completeness of the destruction of such materials', the agreement creates legal risk. Without a strong statement that the receiving party must destroy and certify the completeness of the destruction or return of all confidential information, the disclosing party has no way of knowing whether or not they complied. This opens them up to the risk of confidential information leaking.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted in Tennessee)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Tennessee Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) provides remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets. If a party fails to adequately return or destroy confidential information after a request, and retains copies that are later used to the disclosing party's detriment, this could be construed as misappropriation. The omission of the destruction and certification requirement weakens the protection of trade secrets and increases the risk of violating the UTSA.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the return of all confidential information (including originals, copies, and reproductions) and requires certification of completeness, while the modified text only requires the return of confidential information without these additional safeguards. The Law Explanation states that the omission of the destruction and certification requirement weakens the protection of trade secrets. The Perturbation Explanation confirms that the disclosing party has no way of knowing whether or not they complied without a strong statement that the receiving party must destroy and certify the completeness of the destruction or return of all confidential information. The absence of scraped snippets does not negate the validity of the other provided explanations.
